retrieved_docs.title: Intestinal Fistula -- Treatment / Management -- Conservative or Non-Operative Approach
retrieved_docs.content: Other conservative treatment includes non-operative measures to close the fistula like fibrin glue or other occlusive measures. The success rate of these measures is not high. They are still an option to consider in high-risk patients.

retrieved_docs.title: 2-Octyl Cyanoacrylate -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the indications and contraindications for the usage of 2-octyl cyanoacrylate. Explain how to use 2-octyl cyanoacrylate for wound closure. Summarize the risk of wound infection associated with 2-octyl cyanoacrylate for wound closure with that of suture repair. Review how an interprofessional approach to care can improve the application of 2-octyl cyanoacrylate and render therapy more efficient and effective. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Enterovesical Fistula -- Treatment / Management -- Conservative or Non-operative Approach
retrieved_docs.content: Other conservative treatment includes non-operative measures to close the fistula like fibrin glue or other occlusive measures. The success rate of these measures is not high. They are still an option to consider in high-risk patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Fistula-in-Ano(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Fibrin plug and glue – A fibrin plug is a treatment option that involves a collagen matrix used to block or plug the internal opening of the fistula tract. The treatment is appealing as it does not involve dissection or division of the sphincter complex and therefore, should not contribute to incontinence. Unfortunately, the treatment is less than 50% successful in the treatment of fistula-in-ano. [16] Similarly, fibrin glue has also been trialed to promote the healing of fistula tracts. This also preserves sphincter function, but it has low success rates, ranging from 14% to 69%. [17] [18] Both have been initially discarded as ineffective, but more recent studies have involved a combination of surgical fistula treatment in combination with fibrin plugs and fibrin glue, which may develop into a viable treatment option following more research. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Large Bowel Obstruction -- Treatment / Management -- Alternative Interventions
retrieved_docs.content: Adjuncts and alternative treatments have been used for colonic obstruction. Laser coagulation may facilitate the dilation of benign strictures, but treating the underlying cause is essential. For example, antituberculosis medications are used for an obstruction from tuberculosis. Disimpaction may be attempted, digitally or through an enema, for a nonperforated, obstructing stool impaction. If successful, this is followed by a maintenance bowel regimen. Alternatives to surgery have been described for pathology within the colon, including the use of fibrin glue for fistulae and perforations, but the recurrence rate is high with these modalities. Endoscopic treatments have been applied selectively, including through-the-scope and over-the-scope methods, endoscopic suturing, and tracking systems. [35]

retrieved_docs.title: Bilobed Flaps -- Equipment -- Postoperatively
retrieved_docs.content: Bacitracin ointment, or: Skin glue, or: Adhesive and sterile tape strips

retrieved_docs.title: Small Bowel Resection -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: With the diseased bowel segment now cut and removed, the cut ends of the small bowel are held aligned, and stay sutures are placed in the corners. The clamps holding the bowel ends are rotated in opposition to one another to bring the posterior outer serosal layers together. A series of silk interrupted Lembert sutures are then placed through the seromuscular layers to conjoin the 2 segments. This is continued until the stay sutures are reached at each end. Next, absorbable sutures are placed in full-thickness bites to form the posterior inner layer along with the same distance. The sutures should be placed so that the mucosa inverts and contacts the opposing mucosa throughout. With the posterior layer complete, absorbable Connel (U-shaped) stitches are placed in the corner, and the pattern is continued to close the anterior inner layer. Suturing of the inner layers can be safely done with either interrupted or continuous patterns. Continuous sutures have been shown in animal models to cause decreased perianastomotic tissue oxygen tension compared to interrupted sutures; however, limited human trials have not shown any significant difference in anastomotic leak rates when comparing the 2. Once that anterior inner layer is closed, silk Lembert stitches are placed in interrupted fashion across the anterior outer surface, completing the 2-layer anastomosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Small Bowel Obstruction -- Treatment / Management -- Recurrent Obstructions
retrieved_docs.content: For patients with recurrent SBO, often due to adhesions, preventive strategies may include minimally invasive surgical techniques and adhesion barriers during surgery to reduce the formation of future adhesions.

retrieved_docs.title: Ileal Atresia -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Jejunoileal atresia (JIA) is the most common cause of intestinal obstruction in neonates. It has been generally reported that JIA occurs due to a vascular accident involving mesenteric blood supply in-utero. The atresia can be found anywhere in the small bowel, either as solitary or multiple lesions. Advancement in pediatric surgical techniques, pediatric anesthesia, intensive care unit (ICU) care, and nutritional supports over the years has led to better overall survival in patients with intestinal atresia.

retrieved_docs.title: Single-Anastomosis Duodeno-Ileal Bypass  -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: A leak test is performed by instilling methylene blue or air through an orogastric tube while observing for bubbles or leaks at the anastomosis site in a saline-filled peritoneal cavity. An endoscope can also be used to test for a leak. The abdomen is inspected before deflation to ensure hemostasis has been achieved. Trocars are removed under direct visualization, and the trocar sites are closed, including fascia at the larger trocar sites to prevent incisional hernias. The subcutaneous tissue and skin are approximated according to the surgeon's preference, and a dressing or glue is applied. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Small Bowel Obstruction -- Treatment / Management -- Surgical Management
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical options depend on the underlying cause of SBO. Adhesiolysis is the most common procedure, performed laparoscopically or via open surgery, to release fibrous bands causing the obstruction. For strangulated obstruction, resection of the necrotic bowel may be required, followed by either primary anastomosis or stoma formation, depending on the extent of damage and patient stability. In cases of questionable bowel viability or extensive ischemia, second-look laparotomy or temporary abdominal closure techniques, such as vacuum-assisted closure, may be required. [33] [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Gastric Varices -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: TIPS and BRTO : If acute gastric variceal bleeding is not amenable to be controlled with endoscopic intervention, then transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt (TIPS) or Balloon occluded retrograde transvenous obliteration (BRTO) should be performed TIPS is an invasive technique that involves creating an intrahepatic connection between the hepatic and the portal venous system resulting in the diversion of the portal venous flow into the systemic circulation, thereby reducing portal pressure. It is the treatment of choice in acute gastric variceal bleeding, especially GOV2 and IGV1. Randomized control trials have shown that TIPS has been more effective than cyanoacrylate injection in preventing rebleeding from gastric varices, with similar survival rates and frequency of associated complications. [29] BRTO has emerged as a safe and clinically effective treatment option in managing gastric variceal bleeding and should be considered an alternative option, especially if patients with associated refractory hepatic encephalopathy. BRTO is an invasive technique that involves the retrograde injection of a sclerosing agent into gastric varix after occluding the portosystemic shunt with an occlusion balloon under fluoroscopic guidance. However, the performance of BRTO requires the presence of a spontaneous portosystemic shunt such as a gastro-renal shunt or a gastro-caval which is present in 60 to 85 % of cases and should be predetermined by computed tomography or magnetic resonance imaging prior to the performance of this procedure. A meta-analysis of 24 studies involving 1,016 patients by Park et al. reported the technical success rate for BRTO is 96.4%, and the clinical success rate was 97.3%. [30] Retrospective studies comparing TIPS with BRTO have reported that the active bleeding control rate, rebleeding rate, and complication rate were better with BRTO compared to TIPS. [31] [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Ileal Conduit -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: It is thought to have originally been developed by French surgeons during World War II. Still, the technique did not become prominent until 1950, when an effective adhesive appliance for ileostomy was introduced. As time has passed and oncological and surgical techniques have evolved, selecting the right patients for an ileal conduit has become more refined along with the surgical approach.

retrieved_docs.title: Small Bowel Obstruction -- Prognosis -- Simple SBO
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with simple SBO, where there is no evidence of ischemia or perforation, generally have a favorable prognosis. Nonoperative management resolves most cases of partial or adhesional obstructions without surgery. Recovery is typically rapid if managed appropriately, with mortality rates in these cases being low (1%–5%). However, recurrent SBO remains a challenge, particularly in patients with prior surgeries and adhesions.

retrieved_docs.title: Circumcision -- Technique or Treatment -- Gomco clamp
retrieved_docs.content: Some recommendations include the application of cyanoacrylate tissue adhesive on the cut edge, particularly when this technique is performed on older adults.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 238
retrieved_docs.content: venous procedures such as varicose‑vein ligation and stripping, and anorectal hemorrhoidal treatments through common procedural categories (incision, excision, occlusion), shared anatomical sites (vein, arterial and venous plexuses), and unified clinical objectives of restoring perfusion, relieving venous congestion, and managing hemorrhoidal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1861
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical action is identified as the method_of performing surgical repair and revision of anastomosis, indicating that specific operative actions are employed as the technique used to correct or modify anastomotic connections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2022
retrieved_docs.content: Exploratory laparotomy and ثقب معوي (intestinal perforation) are frequently observed together in clinical practice. An exploratory laparotomy is often performed when a ثقب معوي is suspected or identified, as the surgical approach allows direct visualization and management of the perforated bowel. Recognizing the co‑occurrence of these conditions is important for timely diagnosis and appropriate operative intervention, which can reduce morbidity and improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2745
retrieved_docs.content: Perineal urethrorrhaphy, suture of urethra, suture plication of artery for control of duodenal ulcer hemorrhage, and suture plication of artery for control of rectal hemorrhage are all surgical techniques that involve suturing and require the use of specialized devices. These procedures demonstrate that across urologic, gastrointestinal, and colorectal contexts, suturing interventions commonly depend on appropriate devices to achieve effective tissue approximation and hemostasis, underscoring the importance of device selection in successful surgical outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2655
retrieved_docs.content: The internal rectal venous plexus is identified as the anatomical site for the procedure referred to as ربط الباسور, a rubber‑band ligation technique used to treat internal hemorrhoids. This connection underscores that effective management of hemorrhoidal disease involves applying the ربط الباسور procedure directly to the internal rectal venous plexus, thereby targeting the vascular structures responsible for hemorrhoid formation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2832
retrieved_docs.content: Plication is a surgical technique that involves folding or suturing tissue to achieve structural modification or hemostasis. A Plication of kidney represents a specific application of this technique to the renal parenchyma, often used to reduce renal volume or correct deformities. Suture plication of artery for control of gastric hemorrhage and Suture plication of artery for control of rectal hemorrhage are distinct uses of arterial suture plication to stop bleeding in the gastrointestinal and colorectal regions, respectively. These examples demonstrate the versatility of plication across different organ systems for both tissue remodeling and effective hemorrhage control.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 246
retrieved_docs.content: for hemorrhoids are interconnected through shared categories of vessel incision, occlusion, and excision, common procedural sites (arteries, veins, internal rectal venous plexus), and therapeutic objectives such as blood drainage and vein ligation. Understanding these anatomical sites, procedural hierarchies, and associated pathologies is essential for accurate diagnosis, appropriate therapeutic planning, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2549
retrieved_docs.content: Plication is a surgical technique that encompasses several specific applications, including plication of the kidney, suture plication of an artery to control duodenal ulcer hemorrhage, and suture plication of an artery to control rectal hemorrhage. In each context, the procedure involves suturing tissue to achieve structural reinforcement or hemostasis, demonstrating the versatility of plication across different organ systems and bleeding scenarios.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2161
retrieved_docs.content: Penile urethrorrhaphy, Perineal urethrorrhaphy and Suture of urethra are surgical techniques that involve suturing the urethra and require the use of specialized suturing devices. Likewise, Suture plication of artery for control of duodenal ulcer hemorrhage and Suture plication of artery for control of rectal hemorrhage are arterial suturing methods employed to achieve hemostasis in gastrointestinal bleeding, and they also depend on device‑assisted suturing. These procedures collectively illustrate that both urologic repairs and gastrointestinal hemorrhage control rely on the application of suturing devices to secure tissue and stop bleeding.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 184
retrieved_docs.content: plications) to their broader procedural categories and highlighting the common reliance on suturing devices across these diverse clinical applications. This integrated view aids clinicians in understanding the hierarchy of procedures, selecting appropriate techniques for urethral and other organ repairs, and recognizing the shared technical foundations that underlie successful surgical outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2356
retrieved_docs.content: Penile urethrorrhaphy, suture of urethra, and suture plication of artery for control of rectal hemorrhage are surgical techniques that all involve suturing and require the use of specialized devices to achieve tissue approximation and hemostasis. These procedures demonstrate how suturing devices are employed across different anatomical sites—specifically in urologic repairs of the penile urethra and general urethral injuries, as well as in vascular control to stop rectal hemorrhage—underscoring the importance of appropriate device selection for effective surgical outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 1159
retrieved_docs.content: The internal rectal venous plexus is the anatomical region where hemorrhoids are identified and also serves as the procedural site for the treatment known as ربط الباسور (rubber‑band ligation). Hemorrhoids themselves are categorized as بواسير غير محددة من دون ذكر مضاعفات, meaning they are unspecified hemorrhoids without documented complications. Recognizing the internal rectal venous plexus as both the finding site for hemorrhoids and the location for ربط الباسور helps clinicians accurately diagnose these vascular lesions and select an appropriate minimally invasive intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 126
retrieved_docs.content: type (gastrointestinal surgery, hemorrhoid procedures, vein incisions, varicose‑vein stripping), providing clinicians with a comprehensive framework for managing vascular disorders, hemorrhoidal disease, and lower‑limb varicose‑vein surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 907
retrieved_docs.content: Plication is a surgical technique that involves folding or suturing tissue to achieve structural support or hemostasis. It is applied as a Plication of kidney, as a Suture plication of artery for control of gastric hemorrhage, as a Suture plication of artery for control of rectal hemorrhage, and as Ureteroplication. These varied uses demonstrate the versatility of plication across renal, vascular, gastrointestinal, and urinary procedures, underscoring its importance in controlling bleeding and maintaining organ function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1743
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical procedures on the colon and rectum are classified as a type of gastrointestinal tract surgery, representing a specific subset of abdominal surgical interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2637
retrieved_docs.content: Perineal urethrorrhaphy, prostatomembranous urethrorrhaphy, suture of urethra, and suture plication of artery for control of rectal hemorrhage are surgical procedures that all employ suturing devices to achieve precise tissue approximation and hemostasis. The urethrorrhaphy techniques (perineal and prostatomembranous) are used to reconstruct or repair the urethra, while suture plication of an artery is applied to control rectal hemorrhage. The shared reliance on a suturing device highlights the critical role of accurate suture placement in both urologic reconstruction and vascular bleeding control, promoting effective repair and reducing the risk of postoperative complications.
